blocks.text: Orson Pratt’s 1876 edition marked a major step forward in the publication of the Doctrine and Covenants. Intriguingly, however, it was not the 1876 printing but a revision of that edition three years later that became the familiar standard for the next generation of Latter-day Saints.

Brigham Young’s death less than a year after the 1876 edition appeared brought changes in Church administration. Orson Pratt had left Salt Lake City for England the month before President Young’s passing. He had planned to publish the Doctrine and Covenants there in the Deseret alphabet, a phonetic spelling system developed to help European migrants learn to read English. Elder Pratt’s return to Salt Lake the month after Brigham Young’s death ended that project.

The following year, however, Orson Pratt returned to England to publish the Book of Mormon using electrotype technology. Like the 1876 Doctrine and Covenants, Elder Pratt’s 1879 edition of the Book of Mormon divided the text into the small chapters and verses used thereafter by Latter-day Saints. It also furnished footnotes for cross-reference and gospel study.

While Orson Pratt was in England, he suggested to British Mission president William Budge that the Doctrine and Covenants be reprinted to match the new edition of the Book of Mormon. President Budge wrote to John Taylor, president of the Quorum of the Twelve Apostles, in Salt Lake City to explain Elder Pratt’s idea: “He suggests the idea of having ‘Plates’ for that work from the same Type, and pages of the Same Size as of the ‘Book of Mormon,’ that the two Books may be uniform in length and width.” William Budge’s letter reached Salt Lake City, and the matter was taken under consideration by the Quorum of the Twelve. There was no First Presidency at that point because it had been dissolved at the death of Brigham Young and had not yet been reorganized. In the quorum meeting, apostle Brigham Young Jr. observed that the 1876 Salt Lake edition of the Doctrine and Covenants was about sold out, and the rest of the quorum agreed that it would be a good idea to prepare an electrotype edition in England, keeping one set of plates in Liverpool for printing there and sending another set to Salt Lake City.

The apostles recommended a few improvements to the new electrotype edition. The table of contents in the 1876 edition was very long, with too much detail for most readers. The Quorum of the Twelve suggested replacing it with “a good full index.” The quorum also directed that the book “be printed with marginal references” similar to those prepared for the 1879 Book of Mormon.

Meanwhile, Orson Pratt thought of two more ideas for improving the new edition of the Doctrine and Covenants. In 1851, British Mission president Franklin D. Richards had printed a pamphlet in Liverpool to make some of Joseph Smith’s inspired writings available to Saints in England. President Richards entitled his anthology The Pearl of Great Price. Now, in 1879, Elder Pratt wrote to President Taylor to propose combining the contents of The Pearl of Great Price with the new edition of the Doctrine and Covenants. To make room for it, he recommended deleting the Lectures on Faith, which had been bound with the revelations since the 1835 edition of the Doctrine and Covenants.

Before receiving Elder Pratt’s additional recommendations, however, the Quorum of the Twelve had already made the decision regarding the contents of the new Doctrine and Covenants, and they decided to abide by it. Elder Pratt, of course, deferred to them. He was especially enthusiastic about replacing the table of contents with an index, which had been mostly prepared two years earlier. The decision to add marginal references to the volume, however, weighed heavily on him.

“But alas!” he wrote to President Taylor. “There has not yet been a scratch of the pen—not even a beginning to get up these references. It required over one year’s hard labor to get up the references in the Book of Mormon. I think the references in the Book of Covenants will be equally extensive if not greater than those of the B[ook] of [Mormon].” Elder Pratt agreed the marginal references were “imperatively called for” and would “undoubtedly be highly appreciated by all who love the word of God.” He estimated that completing the references would occupy him until the spring of 1880.

The members of the Quorum of the Twelve were sympathetic to Elder Pratt’s concerns and suggested that he select missionaries in England who were “well acquainted with the Scriptures” to help prepare the references. “Let them perform the labor,” the apostles directed, “and you revise and supervise the work.” They thought that would speed up the project and allow Elder Pratt to “be with us again before the close of the year,” 1879.

Orson Pratt complied with their directions and chose four elders to assist him in the work. With their help, the project progressed rapidly, and the sixty-seven-year-old Elder Pratt felt relieved to have his burden lifted. Speaking for Elder Pratt, mission president William Budge wrote to John Taylor in May 1879, “He thanks you for giving him what help he needs as it will favor him greatly, his eyes being rather weak with so much reading.”

As anticipated by the Quorum of the Twelve, Orson Pratt was able to return to Salt Lake City in September 1879, joining his fellow apostles there in time for the Church’s October general conference. By mid-October, the 1879 electrotype edition of the Doctrine and Covenants was available for sale in England. The second set of electrotype plates eventually made its way to Utah, where the plates were used to print a Salt Lake version of the same edition, which appeared in 1880.

In the October general conference of 1880, a solemn assembly sustained John Taylor as the third president of The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints, with George Q. Cannon as his first counselor and Joseph F. Smith as his second. Each quorum or group stood in turn to vote during the assembly, which was conducted by the stately Orson Pratt, “with hair and full beard, made gloriously white by the frosts of sixty-nine winters.”

Later, the newly sustained first counselor in the First Presidency, George Q. Cannon, stood before the conference with the latest edition of the Doctrine and Covenants, which included the twenty-six sections added by Orson Pratt in 1876, as well as the reference aids added more recently by Elder Pratt at the direction of the Quorum of the Twelve. President Cannon also held up the separately printed Pearl of Great Price, which had been issued in a Utah edition in 1878.

“I hold in my hand the Book of Doctrine and Covenants,” he announced, “and also the book, The Pearl of Great Price, which books contain revelations of God. In Kirtland, the Doctrine and Covenants, in its original form, as first printed, was submitted to the officers of the Church and the members of the Church to vote upon. As there have been additions made to it by the publishing of revelations which were not contained in the original edition, it has been deemed wise to submit these books with their contents to the conference, to see whether the conference will vote to accept the books and their contents as from God, and binding upon us as a people and as a Church.”

After President Cannon made this motion, the new second counselor in the First Presidency, Joseph F. Smith, spoke next. “I move that we receive and accept the revelations contained in these books, as revelations from God to the Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints, and to all the world.”
The conference voted unanimously in the affirmative. The new edition of the Doctrine and Covenants thus began more than four decades as the standard edition of the modern revelations.
